data.title: TPG expects 2026 capital raising to exceed $50B as it targets a 47% FRE margin
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TPG Inc. (TPG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "TPG entered 2026 with strong momentum following a record year of capital formation and deployment," said CEO Jon Winkelried, adding that "our fee-related earnings grew 36% year-over-year and exceeded $1 billion on an LTM basis for the first time in TPG's history" and "total AUM grew 22% to $306 billion."

  • Winkelried framed the macro backdrop as "the convergence of AI disruption, private credit stress and geopolitical conflict" and said, "We view the current environment as an opportunity, and we have never felt more confident in the positioning of our franchise."

  • On AI and software, Winkelried said, "We've evaluated each of our software companies through a framework based on offensive opportunity and defensive risk" and highlighted that "aggregate bookings in our TPG Capital and TPG Growth software portfolio growing more than 20% year-over-year."

  • On private credit positioning, Winkelried said, "our credit portfolios are healthy" and pointed to TCAP flows: "gross inflows of $193 million and redemption requests of $31 million" and said TCAP "ended the quarter with $4.7 billion of AUM, up 33% year-over-year."

  • CFO Jack Weingart said, "We reported fee-related revenue of $557 million in the first quarter" and "Fee-related earnings for the quarter were $247 million" while "Our FRE margin was 44.3% in the quarter."

Outlook

  • Weingart reaffirmed profitability goals, saying, "We remain confident in our ability to achieve a full year 2026 FRE margin of 47%."

  • Management reiterated the annual fundraising target, with Winkelried stating, "Overall, we remain on track to raise more than $50 billion this year," and Weingart adding, "We continue to expect capital raising to exceed $50 billion this year" while noting it is expected to be "weighted toward the back half of the year."

  • Product roadmap emphasis in wealth and credit continued, with Weingart saying, "we're actively working on launching new products, including a nontraded REIT as well as a multi-strategy credit interval fund," and Winkelried adding that clients "have expressed interest in a TPG multi-strategy credit interval fund, which we plan to launch next year."

Financial Results

  • In prepared remarks, Head of IR Gary Stein reported, "a GAAP net loss attributable to TPG Inc. of $123 million" and "after-tax distributable earnings of $282 million or $0.70 per share" and added, "We declared a dividend of $0.59 per share ... which will be paid on May 26."

  • Weingart said AUM growth was supported by capital raising and value creation: "We ended the quarter with $306 billion of total assets under management" and "Our fee-earning AUM grew 23% to $175 billion" while "AUM subject to fee earning growth totaled $45 billion ... including $33 billion of AUM not yet earning fees" and "we have visibility into approximately $140 million of annual revenue opportunity as this capital is put to work."

  • Weingart highlighted balance sheet actions tied to the Jackson partnership: "We used our revolver to fund $500 million investment in Jackson common stock" and later "issued $500 million of senior notes"; he said, "our interest expense increased to $26 million" and "we had $2.3 billion of net debt and $1.7 billion of available liquidity."

  • On realizations and marks, Weingart said, "We generated $68 million in realized performance allocations" and added, "the value of our PE portfolio declined 1%" while "Our credit platform appreciated 2% in the first quarter" and "Our real estate platform appreciated approximately 2% in the first quarter."

Q&A

  • Glenn Schorr, Evercore ISI: "help us think through the marks in PE" and whether they were "a few specific names" and "how much software related"; Chief Financial Officer Weingart: "we did take multiples down broadly, and it was offset by very strong earnings growth" and quantified, "The impact of multiple reductions was negative $2.4 billion" in TPG Capital and "negative $1.1 billion" in growth; President Todd Sisitsky: "we had two strategic exits ... at premiums to our marks."

  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: asked which credit verticals should be "most active" and any "implications on the fee rates"; CEO Winkelried: "the pipeline of opportunities there has never been stronger" for Credit Solutions and highlighted asset-based finance/structured credit as "a very substantial growth area."

  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities: asked if AI enablement meant cross-fund follow-ons and appetite for software opportunities; Sisitsky: "we do not start to cross and come in from new funds" and described recent carve-outs including "Velotic" and "Optum U.K."; Executive Chairman James Coulter said, "By the end of this year, I think we'll probably be talking about offense" on AI.

  • Kenneth Worthington, JPMorgan: asked how large deals translate into capital markets fees and then, "nothing to call out for 2Q?"; Weingart: "Predicting in one quarter is hard" and responded to the follow-up with "No."

  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research: asked about AI-risk review beyond software and whether marks reflected weaker growth; Sisitsky: "it was distinctively just the public marks coming down" and said remaining TPG VIII value was $13.7 billion with "7%" in a "mitigate" category.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly skeptical in spots, led by valuation-mark questions (e.g., Schorr: "I'm going to pick at one issue") and fee/margin probing (Giblat: "I'm just trying to reconcile the potential upside I see here").

  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks, with Winkelried saying, "we have never felt more confident" and Weingart emphasizing "continued growth and margin expansion" while giving specific guardrails on margin and fundraising.

  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call carried more emphasis on mark-to-market and AI-risk framing in Q&A, while the prior quarter featured more explicit pushback on market skepticism (Q4 Schorr: "your stocks fall like rocks") that was not repeated in Q1.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Profitability mix shifted from Q4 profitability to Q1 loss on a GAAP basis, as Stein reported Q4 "GAAP net income ... of $77 million" versus Q1 "GAAP net loss ... of $123 million," while after-tax distributable earnings were $304 million in Q4 versus $282 million in Q1.

  • FRE margin stepped down from a Q4 peak to a more typical level, with Weingart previously reporting "fourth quarter FRE margin reached a record 52%" and now reporting Q1 "FRE margin was 44.3%" while keeping the same full-year target of "approximately 47%"/"47%."

  • Dividend declaration moved lower sequentially, from Q4 "$0.61 per share" to Q1 "$0.59 per share," as disclosed by Stein on each call.

  • Strategic narrative evolved from Q4 announcing the Jackson partnership structure and minimums to Q1 discussing execution, with Winkelried saying the Jackson partnership was "tracking ahead of our plan" and noting "$2 billion of initial commitments" received into asset-based finance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged uncertainty drivers, with Winkelried citing "AI disruption, private credit stress and geopolitical conflict" and Weingart warning that "current market volatility may impact the timing of realizations across the industry."

  • On valuations, Weingart described the mitigation approach as aligning to markets: "we did take multiples down broadly" and said improved markets would be reflected later: "if market conditions continue to improve, we'll reflect those increasing valuation multiples."

  • On retail-oriented credit vehicles, Winkelried acknowledged, "some retail-oriented credit vehicles are experiencing elevated redemptions," while pointing to TCAP’s reported redemption requests as a counterpoint.

Final Takeaway

TPG management described Q1 2026 as a quarter of accelerating platform activity alongside a tougher mark-to-market backdrop: Winkelried emphasized AI-related portfolio positioning, credit resilience, and a continued fundraising target of more than $50 billion for 2026, while Weingart reiterated a 47% full-year FRE margin goal and detailed balance sheet actions tied to the Jackson partnership, alongside a quarter that included a GAAP loss, lower private equity valuations driven by multiple resets, and ongoing credit and real estate appreciation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alliant outlines 7%+ earnings CAGR for 2027-2029 as contracted data center demand reaches 3.4 GW
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alliant Energy (LNT) Q1 2026

Management view

  • CEO Lisa Barton said “2026 is off to an excellent start,” adding that “First quarter ongoing earnings delivered approximately 25% of the midpoint of our full year guidance despite very mild temperatures across our service territory,” and that the company remains “firmly on track to achieve our 2026 earnings targets while executing on our strategic priorities.”

  • CEO Barton highlighted a new large-load contract: “In April, we executed a new 370-megawatt electric service agreement with a hyperscale customer in Iowa with a full load ramp expected by the end of 2030,” and said Alliant “entered into an agreement with a high-quality counterparty to construct a simple cycle natural gas facility” to support that growth.

  • CEO Barton said the company now has “5 fully executed data center agreements representing approximately 3.4 gigawatts of contracted demand with 3 of these projects under active construction,” and framed the scale as “more than a 60% increase in our current peak demand.”

  • CEO Barton tied the data center contracting approach to capital needs and flexibility: “because our more recent electric service agreements are capacity only, the investments required to serve this load are primarily energy storage and natural gas combustion turbines.”

  • CFO Robert Durian reported Q1 profitability: “Yesterday, we announced solid first quarter 2026 GAAP and ongoing earnings of $0.87 and $0.82, respectively,” and said the year-over-year change in ongoing earnings was “primarily due to higher revenue requirements and AFUDC from capital investments at our Iowa and Wisconsin utilities,” offset by higher O&M, depreciation, and financing costs.

Outlook

  • CFO Durian said, “We are reaffirming our 2026 earnings guidance,” while adding, “Our longer-term earnings outlook remains intact,” and “based on our current plan, we expect our compound annual earnings growth rate across 2027 through 2029 to be 7% plus.”

  • CEO Barton guided to more disclosure later in the year on the incremental data center pipeline: “our third quarter earnings call and EEI, we will be providing that full update of our resource plan, which would include providing the generation necessary to support the 370 megawatts, an update on our EPS and growth trajectory.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management again used the same long-term framing around growth, with CFO Durian reiterating “7% plus” for 2027-2029, while the current quarter added a specific near-term catalyst in a newly executed “370-megawatt electric service agreement.”

Financial results

  • CFO Durian stated temperature impacts: “Temperatures in the first quarter of 2026 reduced electric and gas margins by approximately $0.04 per share compared to a reduction of $0.03 in the prior year,” and added, “Excluding the impacts of temperatures, electric sales in the first quarter were essentially even year-over-year.”

  • CFO Durian described an item excluded from ongoing earnings: “First quarter ongoing earnings exclude a $0.05 benefit from the remeasurement of deferred tax assets, reflecting updated state income tax apportionment assumptions driven by higher projected electric utility revenues from commercial and industrial customers, including data centers.”

  • CFO Durian detailed Q1 financing execution and remaining 2026 plans: “during the first quarter of 2026, we had parent level and Alliant Energy Finance maturities of $1.1 billion,” which were retired “including a $400 million term loan,” and he said remaining 2026 debt plans include “up to $800 million of long-term issuances, consisting of up to $300 million at WPL and up to $500 million at IPL.”

  • CFO Durian updated equity funding actions: “of the approximately $2.4 billion of expected common equity needs over the next 4 years, we have already raised approximately $1.3 billion through forward equity agreements,” leaving “approximately $1 billion of remaining equity to be raised through 2029,” and added, “A new $1 billion at-the-market program was filed during the first quarter.”

Q&A

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo: asked whether Alliant could provide “EPS disclosures” and a “more definable EPS guidance range” as data center opportunities “start to accrete”; President, CEO & Director Barton responded, “our third quarter earnings call and EEI, we will be providing… an update on our EPS and growth trajectory.”

  • Pourreza, Wells Fargo: asked about “noise in Wisconsin” and whether hyperscalers are shifting to Iowa; President, CEO & Director Barton said, “conversations do continue in Wisconsin,” while noting, “there’s rhetoric that’s out there… spillover… from PJM,” and “we have our customer pledge, making sure that everybody knows that they are not paying for data centers.”

  • Nicholas Campanella, Barclays: asked for simple-cycle cost per kW; President, CEO & Director Barton said, “We cannot disclose the cost due to confidentiality agreements,” and added, “you can expect those to be in line with what you’re seeing in the marketplace today.”

  • William Appicelli, UBS: asked about resource mix and MISO accreditation; President, CEO & Director Barton said the mix is “primarily batteries and peakers,” and on timing added, “we’ll have a cleaner line of sight as we get closer to Q3.”

  • Appicelli, UBS: asked CT sizing; President, CEO & Director Barton said, “it basically 1.1 gigawatts,” then clarified “Up to.”

  • Andrew Weisel, Scotiabank: asked in-service date for the new CT; President, CEO & Director Barton answered, “’31.”

  • Stephen D’Ambrisi, RBC: asked whether incremental load extends Iowa’s ability to “stay out” of base rate cases longer; Executive VP & CFO Durian said, “think of it as incrementally better,” but added, “we’re not in a position right now to give you any kind of definitive time frame.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive but pressing on quantification and execution risk, repeatedly asking for incremental economics and disclosures (e.g., Pourreza, Wells Fargo: “do we ever get to a point where we could see a more definable EPS guidance range”).

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was positive and confident on execution cadence and strategy (President, CEO & Director Barton: “2026 is off to an excellent start”), while responses in Q&A were more guarded on specifics, frequently citing confidentiality (President, CEO & Director Barton: “We cannot disclose the cost due to confidentiality agreements”).

  • Versus last quarter, analyst questioning remained centered on data center timing and Wisconsin siting dynamics; this quarter added more detailed probing on generation sizing, MISO accreditation impacts, and the timeline for combustion turbines.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current quarter expanded contracted load disclosure from “4 executed ESAs totaling 3 gigawatts” (prior quarter) to “5 fully executed data center agreements… approximately 3.4 gigawatts,” and added a new “370-megawatt electric service agreement” with a ramp “by the end of 2030.”

  • Financing commentary shifted from planned issuances to actions taken: in Q4 the company outlined “up to $1.2 billion of long-term issuances” for 2026, while in Q1 management reported $1.1B of maturities addressed and said remaining 2026 long-term issuances are “up to $800 million.”

  • Regulatory posture remained consistent on “no active rate reviews planned in 2026,” while the current quarter added new docket activity including “1 active Iowa docket for a 720-megawatt natural gas combustion turbine project” and “5 active Wisconsin dockets.”

Risks and concerns

  • Management flagged external process uncertainty around reliability accreditation and interconnection policy, with President, CEO & Director Barton noting MISO is “still working on some of that,” and on FERC policy saying, “We are anxiously waiting… but no line of sight on that.”

  • Wisconsin siting and public pushback remained a topic, with President, CEO & Director Barton referencing “local pushback and moratoriums on new data center developments” and saying the company is “actively addressing countering that” while emphasizing customer cost protections.

Final takeaway

Alliant Energy management positioned Q1 as on-track against reaffirmed 2026 guidance, while emphasizing accelerating large-load contracting, including a new 370 MW Iowa agreement and a total of about 3.4 GW across five executed data center ESAs. Management linked the incremental load to a resource strategy centered on batteries and simple-cycle combustion turbines, with an update planned for Q3 alongside a refreshed Iowa resource plan and an “update on our EPS and growth trajectory,” while maintaining the longer-term “7% plus” earnings growth outlook for 2027-2029.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Barclays turns neutral on risk assets as Hormuz closure persists
data.description:
Barclays Global Chairman of Research Ajay Rajadhyaksha said Friday he has downgraded his outlook on risk assets to neutral, citing accelerating declines in global oil (USO) (BNO) inventories as the cushion that supported his earlier bullish stance erodes.

In a Bloomberg interview, Rajadhyaksha explained that the initial three-week buffer following the Strait of Hormuz disruption has been exhausted. Global inventories are now drawing down at 80M to 90M barrels per week, with outages running at 10M to 12M barrels daily.

“The first three weeks, you still had tankers that had left Hormuz that were landing on shores,” Rajadhyaksha said. “That cushion is gone.”

The strategist had previously maintained a bullish stance, arguing that markets underestimated the buffers held by major economies. He pointed to China’s (MCHI) 1.2B barrels in strategic reserves and the U.S.'s (SPY) status as an energy superpower, along with roughly 700M barrels of inventory built up last year.

Despite his more cautious view, Rajadhyaksha stopped short of recommending outright shorts on risk assets, noting that strong corporate earnings continue to provide support.

He favors the “Mag Six”—the major technology companies excluding Tesla (TSLA)—and two-year Treasuries (SHY) (US2Y), while warning investors away from long-duration bonds (ZROZ) (US30Y) globally.

data.title: UMH targets 800+ new rental homes and tightens 2026 normalized FFO guidance to $0.98-$1.04 per share
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UMH Properties (UMH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Samuel Landy (President, CEO & Director) said the quarter produced “solid operational results,” while earnings were “impacted by increased interest rates and increased investment in rental units and expansion lots, which are not yet occupied,” alongside “seasonal headwinds.” He reported operational improvement, including occupancy growth and “same-property NOI grew by 7%,” and said, “As we continue to fill rental homes and generate increased sales profits, our earnings should increase in the quarters to come.”

  • Samuel Landy (President, CEO & Director) tightened full-year guidance and framed the drivers as occupancy, rentals, and developed sites becoming productive. He said, “We are tightening our NFFO guidance range to $0.98 to $1.04 per share,” and added, “We are well positioned to fill 800 or more new rental homes this year.”

  • Anna Chew (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Director) said, “Normalized FFO, which excludes amortization and nonrecurring items, was $19.4 million or $0.23 per diluted share for the first quarter of 2026 compared to $18.8 million or $0.23 per diluted share for 2025,” and added, “Rental and related income for the quarter was $59.5 million compared to $54.6 million a year ago.”

Outlook

  • Samuel Landy (President, CEO & Director) said, “We are tightening our NFFO guidance range to $0.98 to $1.04 per share or $1.01 per share at the midpoint compared to our previous guidance of $0.97 to $1.05 per share,” and added that he remained “confident in our ability to deliver full year normalized FFO per share growth in the mid-single-digit range.”

  • Management’s 2026 operational priorities included adding rentals and bringing expansion lots online; Samuel Landy (President, CEO & Director) said the company planned to “develop 300 or more sites in 2026” and highlighted “$45 million invested in 600 vacant, well-located expansion sites” that “have been paid for.”

  • The provided analysts estimates data did not include a valid quarter format (Q1–Q4) and did not include EPS estimates, so no comparison to consensus estimates is included.

Financial Results

  • Anna Chew (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Director) reported community-level profitability and cost pressures, saying community NOI “increased by 7% for the quarter from $32.6 million in 2025 to $34.9 million in 2026,” while “community operating expenses increased 10% during the quarter,” driven by acquisitions, “payroll and related costs,” “real estate taxes,” and “water and sewer expenses.”

  • Anna Chew (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Director) detailed leverage and liquidity, including “approximately $760 million in debt” with “99% fixed rate” and a “weighted average interest rate of 4.92%,” plus “$37.4 million in cash and cash equivalents” and “$260 million available on our unsecured revolving credit facility,” which “expires in November” and is under renewal work.

  • Anna Chew (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Director) said the company issued “66,000 shares of our Series D preferred stock” at “a weighted average price of $22.51 per share,” for “$1.5 million” of proceeds, and said the company did not sell common shares under its common ATM during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Gaurav Mehta, Alliance Global Partners: asked whether the company still expected “high single-digit and low double-digit” same-property NOI growth despite winter-driven expenses; Brett Taft (Executive VP & COO) said, “we expect that expense growth to moderate,” and added, “we're absolutely confident in our ability to deliver high single-digit same-property NOI growth.”

  • Gaurav Mehta, Alliance Global Partners: asked about April home sales trends; Brett Taft (Executive VP & COO) said, “Our April sales were very strong. They're coming in at about $3.5 million,” and added, “we remain confident in our ability to grow sales in the second quarter and year-over-year.”

  • Craig Kucera, Lucid Capital Markets: asked about a “significant swing” in the marketable securities portfolio; Anna Chew (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Director) said, “We had written off one security,” and “we moved it from the unrealized to the realized.”

  • Craig Kucera, Lucid Capital Markets: asked about materials sourcing and supply chain risk; Brett Taft (Executive VP & COO) said manufacturers’ backlogs were still “in that 6- to 8-week range,” and Samuel Landy (President, CEO & Director) said, “everything I've heard about supply has remained no issues and no material increases.”

  • Craig Kucera, Lucid Capital Markets: asked how UMH would fund its 2026 budget; Anna Chew (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Director) said, “we always need about $120 million to $150 million on an annual basis,” and cited cash, “$260 million” revolver availability (with “accordion feature” to “$500 million”), and “approximately 60 communities that are free and clear,” while noting it “depends on our share price” and “interest rates.”

  • John Massocca, B. Riley Securities: asked how removing the chassis requirement could impact UMH; Samuel Landy (President, CEO & Director) said, “The removal of the chassis will allow 2-story homes,” and added, “Removing the chassis reduces the cost of each unit by $3,000 or more, but then there's increased setup costs.”

  • Jeffrey Carr, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked for a portfolio occupancy target and where upside may be; Brett Taft (Executive VP & COO) said, “I don't see any reason why... the end of the year, we can't get above 90% occupancy,” and cited “430 homes on site” as “low-hanging fruit.”

  • Jeffrey Carr, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked whether Q1 interest expense was a peak; Anna Chew (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Director) said, “I believe that it is pretty much the same that we will expect throughout the year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, centered on normalization of winter-driven expenses, funding capacity, and interest expense persistence, including questions on “puts and takes on interest expense” and whether expense trends could sustain targeted same-property NOI growth.

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more assertive in Q&A on operating momentum and occupancy; Brett Taft (Executive VP & COO) said, “we're absolutely confident,” and Samuel Landy (President, CEO & Director) said, “we remain incredibly optimistic.”

  • Compared with Q4 2025, the current call placed more emphasis on near-term weather impacts and the timing of earnings accretion from recently funded rentals and expansion lots, while maintaining a similar confidence level around occupancy gains and rental home execution.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from initiating 2026 NFFO guidance in Q4 2025 (“$0.97 to $1.05 per share”) to tightening the range in Q1 2026 (“$0.98 to $1.04 per share”).

  • Development targets moderated from Q4 2025 commentary that entitlements “should allow us to develop 400 or more sites in 2026” to Q1 2026 planning to “develop 300 or more sites in 2026.”

  • Analyst focus moved from Q4 2025 emphasis on the mix of rentals versus sales and the conservatism of guidance to Q1 2026 emphasis on winter cost impacts, April sales pace, securities portfolio accounting, and the trajectory of interest expense.

Risks and Concerns

  • Samuel Landy (President, CEO & Director) cited “increased interest rates” and interest costs from refinancing and from carrying “rental units and expansion lots, which are not yet occupied” as pressures on near-term earnings.

  • Brett Taft (Executive VP & COO) described operational risk from severe winter conditions, including “deep freezes” affecting “water and sewer,” “maintenance overtime,” and “snow removal-related charges,” while stating, “we expect that expense growth to moderate.”

  • Anna Chew (Executive VP, CFO, Treasurer & Director) flagged refinancing and liquidity execution items, including that the unsecured revolver “expires in November” and the company was “currently working on a renewal of this facility.”

Final Takeaway

UMH management said Q1 results reflected improving occupancy and same-property performance, offset by higher interest costs and winter-driven expenses, and positioned 2026 as a year where previously funded rental homes and expansion sites increasingly convert into revenue. The company tightened full-year normalized FFO guidance to $0.98–$1.04 per share, reiterated a goal to fill 800 or more new rental homes, and emphasized the opportunity to push occupancy above 90% as homes already on site and in setup come online, while expecting interest expense to remain relatively steady through the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Federated Hermes outlines $0.11 Q2 EPS impact from FCP deal costs while expecting $12M in added Q2 revenue
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Federated Hermes (FHI) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We ended Q1 with record assets under management of $907 billion, led by gains in Equity and Money Market strategies," said (President, CEO & Chairman John Donahue), while also noting Equity assets ended "at a record high of $101 billion" and Money Market assets ended at "$685 billion."
  • "Gross Equity sales reached a record high of $9.1 billion in Q1," (President, CEO & Chairman Donahue) said, adding that "MDT Equity and Market-Neutral strategies together had a record $5.8 billion of gross sales and over $3.5 billion in net sales in Q1."
  • (President, CEO & Chairman Donahue) highlighted a near-term funding pipeline and mandate dynamics, saying the firm began Q2 with "about $1.1 billion in net institutional mandates yet to fund" and that "Equity strategies are expected to have net redemptions of about $1.4 billion" driven by "net Global Equity expected redemptions of $3 billion" tied to a client decision "to internalize the management of these assets" that was "not driven by performance."
  • On Money Markets, (President, CEO & Chairman Donahue) said the firm’s estimate of mutual fund market share "was about 6.9% at the end of Q1, down from 7.0% at the end of 2025," while also framing tokenization as operational modernization: "We are focused on this area as an infrastructure evolution, not a speculative asset class." He described an "upcoming launch of our Money Market management digital treasury fund" designed to support "both traditional and on-chain distribution" and referenced participation in a "collaborative initiative between BNY and Goldman Sachs" involving "mirrored tokenization of Money Market fund shares."
  • "For Q1 compared to the prior quarter, total revenue decreased $3.9 million, or 1%," said (VP, Treasurer, CFO & Director Thomas Donahue), citing "$10.5 million of lower revenue" from fewer days and noting Q4 included "$8.2 million of Real Estate development fees." He also said Q1 operating expenses "increased by $5.4 million, or 2%" mainly from "seasonally higher compensation and related expenses of $8.5 million."

Outlook

  • (VP, Treasurer, CFO & Director Donahue) quantified expected Q2 acquisition-related items, stating: "additional FCP transaction and related costs incurred in Q2 already include $6.2 million in purchase price treated as compensation and related expense, and $4.6 million of primarily FCP lender consent fees recorded in Professional Service Fees," for "a total estimated transaction-related EPS impact of $0.11 for Q2."
  • (VP, Treasurer, CFO & Director Donahue) added: "we expect that FCP will add approximately $12 million in revenue and $11 million in operating expenses" in Q2, including "a preliminary estimate of $3.8 million of intangible-asset-related expense for Q2."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, the acquisition discussion shifted from an expected closing timeline and broad cost estimate to post-close integration and a specific quarter impact; in Q4, management discussed transaction costs in 2026 and a closing "expected to be in Q2," while in Q1 it detailed the Q2 EPS impact and run-rate expectations.

Financial results

  • (VP, Treasurer, CFO & Director Donahue) said Q1 carried interest and performance fees were "$388,000 compared to $1.6 million in the prior quarter" and that "approximately $283,000 of the Q1 fees were offset by compensation expense."
  • (VP, Treasurer, CFO & Director Donahue) reported the effective tax rate was "26.1%" and reiterated: "We estimate the tax rate to be in the 25% to 28% range for 2026."
  • (VP, Treasurer, CFO & Director Donahue) reported liquidity and capital return actions, saying: "At the end of Q1, cash and investments were $645 million" and "cash and investments, excluding the portion attributable to non-controlling interests, were $607 million." He also said: "During Q1, we purchased 1.2 million shares of FHI stock for $66 million" and "In April, we used $216 million in cash and $23.1 million in FHI Class B stock" for the FCP acquisition, while the board declared "a dividend of $0.38" that "increased $0.04, up nearly 12% from the previous quarter."

Q&A

  • Kenneth Worthington, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: asked what portion of clients will use tokenized money market funds and the long-term share of tokenized cash; (President, CEO & Chairman Donahue) responded "it’s a very low percentage of the clients that are asking for" tokenized products today and described it as "a grand effort to get ready for tomorrow," while (Executive VP & Chief Investment Officer of Money Markets Deborah Cunningham) said she expects "less than 25% of Retail customers" but "25% to 50% utilization" for institutional users over time.
  • Kenneth Worthington, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: asked timing and fee level of the $3 billion global equity withdrawal; (President, CEO & Chairman Donahue) said "that’s probably a Q2 departure" and "the fees on that were lower than the average bear."
  • Robin Holby, TD Cowen: asked about FCP’s next fund launch and real assets demand; (VP, Treasurer, CFO & Director Donahue) said FCP is investing in Fund VI and "won’t start Fund VII" until later, estimating "maybe midyear in 2027," while adding the firm is "pretty excited" about the eventual launch.
  • Robin Holby, TD Cowen: asked whether MDT ETFs are attracting new customers; (President, CEO & Chairman Donahue) said distribution is "a combination of old Intermediaries, new intermediaries" and pointed to growing RIA engagement and increased "Portfolio Construction" activity.
  • Patrick Davitt, Autonomous Research: asked for updated money fund growth expectations given rates and flow noise; (Chief Investment Officer Cunningham) said "’26... is going to be more in the single-digit growth area" and cited indicative yields including "Government yields, 3.72% to 3.75-ish" and "Prime yields, 3.86% to 3.90%," adding money fund industry assets peaked around "$7.5 trillion to $8.2 trillion" and she expects a steady move "over the $8 trillion range." Davitt also asked whether the AUM figure included a late-month spike; (VP, Treasurer, CFO & Director Donahue) replied: "No. The AUM number we gave would have been as of Wednesday."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly skeptical on adoption timelines and flow volatility, with repeated probing on tokenization demand and money fund growth cadence; management acknowledged limited current demand, with (President, CEO & Chairman Donahue) saying tokenized products are not "the usual customer-demand" situation.
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks around record AUM and equity momentum, while pragmatic in Q&A on tokenization and mandate withdrawals; (President, CEO & Chairman Donahue) described tokenization readiness as "protecting our franchise" with "a little FOMO."
  • Versus Q4, the tone around digital assets remained forward-leaning but became more explicit about low present-day client pull, while acquisition tone moved from anticipated closing to quantified costs and integration specifics.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 emphasized record AUM ($907B) and detailed segment net sales dynamics, including a client-driven $3B global equity internalization; Q4 emphasized full-year momentum (including record gross equity sales for 2025) and a larger institutional mandate pipeline figure.
  • Digital assets discussion evolved from "a robust pipeline" and GENIUS-related development language in Q4 to a dual-track product structure in Q1, including a "nontokenized GENIUS compliant structure" plus an intended on-chain share class once infrastructure is available.
  • Capital allocation messaging became more concrete in Q1 with executed buybacks, the closed FCP payment, and a dividend increase, compared with Q4’s forward-looking funding plan for the acquisition and seasonal revenue/day-count expectations.

Risks and concerns

  • (President, CEO & Chairman Donahue) flagged expected equity net redemptions tied to a single institutional client internalizing management, stating it would drive "net Global Equity expected redemptions of $3 billion" and that these were "not driven by performance."
  • (VP, Treasurer, CFO & Director Donahue) highlighted near-term acquisition-related costs, including lender consent fees and purchase-price compensation treatment, and quantified a Q2 "EPS impact of $0.11" from transaction-related items.
  • (President, CEO & Chairman Donahue) described tokenized money market demand as limited today, saying it is "a very low percentage" of clients currently requesting it, while stressing long-term readiness and trust as prerequisites.

Final takeaway

Management’s Q1 message combined record AUM and strong equity sales momentum with a clearer set of near-term offsets: an expected Q2 institutional global equity withdrawal and a quantified Q2 EPS impact tied to the newly closed FCP acquisition. Executives framed digital asset work as infrastructure preparation with low current demand but ongoing product buildout, while the CFO detailed transaction-related costs and incremental Q2 revenue/expense expectations from FCP alongside continued capital returns through buybacks and a higher dividend.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tariff refunds are a divisive issue for Wall Street and Washington. Apple may appease both.
data.description:
After the U.S. Supreme Court invalidated the Trump Administration's usage of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to impose tariffs (without Congressional approval) in February, the administration started accepting claims last month from importers seeking refunds. 

If all tariff refund requests are granted, it could become one of the largest trade-related repayments in U.S. history, to the tune of around $166B, the New York Times recently reported.

Naturally, the topic has been discussed by investors and companies, who want to see if they can reclaim their share of the money.

Levi Strauss (LEVI) recently said it expects around $80M in refunds, while Gap (GAP) CFO Katrina O’Connell said her company is “definitely working on gaining clarity on whether we can recognize a refund.”

Auto giant GM (GM) even went so far as to up its fiscal outlook on the basis that it could receive as much as $500M in refunds.

And while that may be music to the ears of some companies and investors, President Trump has sung a different tune. 

Speaking on CNBC last month, President Trump said he would “remember” U.S. companies that do not seek refunds for the tariffs he unilaterally imposed, even though the Supreme Court ruled they were illegal in a 6-3 decision.

“Brilliant if they don’t do that,” Trump told the news outlet when asked about companies potentially avoiding refund requests to avoid offending him.

Major companies, such as Amazon (AMZN), have not yet filed requests for refunds, prompting Trump to say he was “very honored” by the decision. “If they don’t do that, they’ve got to know me very well,” Trump said further.

He added the Supreme Court ruling did not include language stating, “You don’t have to pay back tariffs that have already been received.” “So, I’m not happy with the Supreme Court, I’ll be honest with you,” he said.

Where Apple comes in

As one of Earth's largest global companies, Apple (AAPL) has been impacted by the tariffs. The tech giant and its manufacturing partners build its products, including iPhones, iPads, and Macs, all over the world, in countries such as China, India, and others.

And while the company has upped its investment in American manufacturing, it has also worked to produce more of its products in the U.S., including the Mac mini, which is being built in Houston.

Nevertheless, it has paid a steep price for President Trump's tariffs. Tariff-related costs totaled $4.2B for the final three quarters of fiscal 2026 and the first quarter of fiscal 2026, according to data compiled by Seeking Alpha.

Apple did not say what its tariff expenses were in the second fiscal quarter, only that they were lower than the previous quarter. 

Apple's plan

Apple appears likely to seek a refund for tariff costs, as evidenced by comments made by CEO Tim Cook.

However, the tech giant's plan for the money may wind up appeasing both Wall Street and President Trump. 

“In terms of applying for a refund of tariffs paid, we're following the established processes, and we plan to reinvest any amount we receive back into U.S. innovation and advanced manufacturing,” Cook said on the earnings call. “These would be new investments and would be in addition to our prior commitments in the U.S.”

President Trump recently praised Cook after his announcement to step down as CEO and said he would “continue to do great work for Apple.”

Apple upped its investment in the U.S. to $600B (over a period of four years) in August 2025, after previously saying it would spend $500B.

Given the close ties between this White House and Apple, turning the tariff refund into a competitive advantage and something that President Trump can champion politically may be one of the wisest decisions the tech giant can make.

data.title: LyondellBasell targets $500m incremental cash flow in 2026 as Middle East disruptions reshape petrochemicals markets
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (LYB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO & Executive Director Peter Z. Vanacker said the company is responding to a conflict-driven supply shock, stating, “This situation in the Middle East has materially disrupted global energy and petrochemicals markets,” and adding, “We expect the impacts will extend beyond the end of the year with much of the world's petrochemical capacity constrained or shut down.”

  • Vanacker linked near-term actions to operations and cost programs, saying, “Supported by our operational excellence and the work from our value enhancement program, we are increasing production to meet this demand,” while also noting “deliberate actions to further streamline our fixed costs” and that the company is “maintaining our discipline on capital expenditures.”

  • Vanacker highlighted a portfolio milestone and leadership change: “We reached another significant milestone in our portfolio transformation by completing the sale of four European assets,” and said David Kinney is retiring, with David Dennison succeeding him as Head of Investor Relations.

  • Executive VP & CFO Agustin Izquierdo emphasized cash conversion and a capital allocation reset: “Over the past 12 months, LyondellBasell converted EBITDA into cash at a rate of 111%, well above our long-term target of 80%,” and “Our Board approved a 50% reduction in our quarterly dividend to rebalance our capital allocation and improve financial flexibility.”

  • Executive Vice President of Global Olefins & Polyolefins Kimberly Foley described a tightening polyolefins backdrop tied to the conflict, saying “more than 20% of the global capacity for ethylene, polyethylene and polypropylene is currently impacted by the ongoing conflict,” and adding, “Our order books are strong with April orders for polyethylene 20% above pre-war averages.”

Outlook

  • Management characterized the disruption as lasting beyond a typical quarter: CEO & Executive Director Peter Z. Vanacker said, “We see that this disruption is not to be measured in quarters... It's probably going to be multiple quarters,” and added that logistics normalization is commonly viewed as “more like... 9 months, 12 months.”

  • Foley outlined near-term pricing actions and planned utilization, stating, “We have announced substantial price increases... including a cumulative $0.50 per pound in polyethylene across April and May... and $0.10 per pound polypropylene spread increases in both months,” and said the company expects “90% utilization of our nameplate capacity across the segment during the second quarter” for O&P Americas.

  • Executive Vice President of Intermediates & Derivatives and Supply Chain Aaron Ledet said Bayport downtime is a near-term swing factor: “The Bayport PO/TBA asset is expected to restart toward the end of the second quarter with an estimated earnings impact of approximately $25 million per week while down.”

  • On Technology, CEO & Executive Director Vanacker said Q1 was below prior expectations but guided to sequential improvement: “We expect improved results in the second quarter... As a result, we estimate that the second quarter Technology segment results will be only slightly lower than our fourth quarter 2025 results.”

Financial Results

  • The company reported “earnings were $0.49 per diluted share with EBITDA of $615 million” in Q1 2026 (CEO & Executive Director Peter Z. Vanacker).

  • Izquierdo said the quarter reflected a seasonal working-capital pattern and intentional rebuild into Q2: “We consumed $269 million of cash in operating activities... consistent with normal patterns for the first quarter,” and “In the second quarter, we expect higher prices and operating rates will result in an intentional build of working capital to capture market opportunities.”

  • Segment highlights included O&P Americas EBITDA of “$327 million, double the prior quarter” (Executive Vice President of Global Olefins & Polyolefins Kimberly Foley) and Intermediates & Derivatives EBITDA of “$224 million” with Bayport downtime reducing EBITDA “by approximately $40 million” (Executive Vice President Ledet).

  • Vanacker said Technology EBITDA was “$18 million” and attributed the shortfall to “declining licensing activity” and “shipping constraints associated with the Middle East war.”

Q&A

  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank: asked why consultants were “too bearish on PE prices in the back half of the year”; CEO & Executive Director Vanacker said, “we see that this disruption is not to be measured in quarters,” and Foley added, “without any correction to the supply-demand imbalance, I'm not sure why pricing would go down... So I politely disagree with the consultants.”

  • Patrick Cunningham, Citi: asked about I&D structural cost-curve changes and back-half utilization; Executive Vice President Ledet said, “we have pricing power across the board,” cited methanol moving “from $300 a ton to $600 a ton,” and said post-restart the company expects to run “closer to 95% to 100% full rates.”

  • Patrick Fischer, Goldman Sachs: asked about potential demand destruction and PE vs. PP dynamics; CEO & Executive Director Vanacker said “the vast majority of polyethylene is going into consumables,” while Foley said “the sleeping giant will be polypropylene.”

  • Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan: asked why Asia PE prices lag higher naphtha; Foley cited China’s “built-in pricing buffer,” crude inventories, and coal-to-olefins setting a floor, while Vanacker added licensing demand is “the lowest since... 15 years.”

  • Vincent Andrews, Morgan Stanley: asked about Europe profitability after the asset sale; CEO & Executive Director Vanacker said the sold scope was “small or even negative,” cited “about EUR 110 million per year in CapEx” reduction and “about EUR 400 million per year” fixed-cost reduction tied to scope reduction, and referenced a potential “earn-out... of EUR 100 million.”

  • John Roberts, Mizuho: asked about APS pricing catch-up; Executive Vice President of Advanced Polymer Solutions Torkel Rhenman said non-contracted pricing moved “aggressively,” but “it's mostly the quarterly part where there is a delay.”

  • Frank Mitsch, Fermium: asked about I&D operating rates and prior guidance; Executive Vice President Ledet cautioned, “95% to 100%, I wouldn't necessarily use that,” but said, “we will be running at benchmark rates across the board.”

  • Mike Sison, Wells Fargo: asked if polypropylene can turn positive; CEO & Executive Director Vanacker said “the market dynamic has completely changed,” and Foley said PP has been running “70% to 75% for the last 2 years” with “15% to 20% operating rate improvement opportunity.”

  • Joshua Spector, UBS: asked why Technology should improve near-term; CEO & Executive Director Vanacker said, “it is a lag,” and “it is definitely not related to having a higher demand.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to pressing, focusing on sustainability of pricing, regional dislocations, and whether higher prices could “destroy demand” (Patrick Fischer, Goldman Sachs).

  • Management tone was positive and assertive in prepared remarks about flexibility and advantage, while more emphatic in Q&A when pushing back on bearish forecasts, including “I politely disagree with the consultants” (Executive Vice President Foley) and “we remain confident” framing around resilience (CEO & Executive Director Vanacker).

  • Versus last quarter’s “challenging and longest downturn” framing (CEO & Executive Director Vanacker, Q4 2025), the current call’s tone shifted toward opportunity from disruption, with repeated emphasis that impacts will persist “many quarters.”

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The dominant narrative shifted from cycle-bottom cost control and depressed margins in Q4 2025 to conflict-driven tightness, supply disruption, and rapid price/margin upside in Q1 2026, including management pointing to unexpected near-term polyolefins pricing actions.

  • Capital allocation messaging changed materially: in Q4 2025, CEO & Executive Director Vanacker said the dividend was being evaluated by the Board; in Q1 2026, Executive VP & CFO Izquierdo said, “Our Board approved a 50% reduction in our quarterly dividend.”

  • The European divestiture moved from “on track for completion in the second quarter of 2026” (CEO & Executive Director Vanacker, Q4 2025) to “completing the sale of four European assets” (CEO & Executive Director Vanacker, Q1 2026).

  • Q4 2025 analyst questions emphasized dividend policy, CapEx baselines, China “anti-involution,” and working capital rebuild; Q1 2026 questions concentrated on the durability of polyolefins pricing, regional price gaps, and operating-rate upside once outages are resolved.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO & Executive Director Vanacker warned of “second order impacts like demand destruction for discretionary spending, especially if oil prices remain at recent highs,” while framing mitigation through “cost-advantaged” assets and ramping “U.S. capacity.”

  • Unplanned downtime was a near-term operational risk, with Executive Vice President Ledet stating Bayport downtime reduced Q1 EBITDA “by approximately $40 million,” with restart targeted “toward the end of the second quarter.”

  • Advanced Polymer Solutions faced margin pressure from input-cost pass-through timing: Executive Vice President Rhenman said “contractual limits on pricing velocity will pressure margins over the near term.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a pivot from a prolonged downturn toward a disruption-driven tightening across petrochemical chains, with LyondellBasell increasing production, pushing through significant polyolefins price actions, and prioritizing balance-sheet flexibility following a 50% dividend reduction. The company tied its outlook to a multi-quarter conflict impact, highlighted the completed sale of four European assets as a portfolio milestone, and positioned near-term earnings sensitivity around operating-rate uplift and the Bayport restart, while noting Technology improvement is expected to come from milestone timing rather than renewed demand.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CubeSmart signals gradual improvement in 2026 with $250M CBRE IM joint venture mandate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CubeSmart (CUBE) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Christopher Marr (CEO, President & Trustee) said Q1 “showed a continuation of trends from late last year with results that were in line with our expectations,” adding, “We are encouraged to finally see the inflection in same-store revenue growth this quarter.”

  • Marr highlighted operating momentum going into peak season, saying “steady demand, when combined with fewer vacates, resulted in a 240% increase in net rentals for the quarter,” and that this “help[ed] to narrow the year-over-year occupancy gap to now 20 basis points by the end of April.”

  • Marr emphasized market bifurcation and early recovery in supply-impacted regions: “Our more stable urban markets in the Northeast and Midwest continue to outperform, while our more transient supply-impacted markets across the Sunbelt and the West Coast are beginning to see green shoots.”

  • Timothy Martin (CFO & Treasurer) attributed the quarter’s margin pressure to expense trends, stating, “Same-store operating expenses grew 5.8% over last year,” including weather impacts: “those elevated [snow removal] costs accounted for about 120 basis points of our overall quarterly same-store expense growth.”

  • Martin framed capital allocation around relative valuation and external growth options, saying, “We again repurchased shares in the quarter as the relative value of our portfolio made it our most attractive investment option,” and added, “We also closed on the first store in our recently announced new joint venture with CBRE IM with a $250 million mandate to invest in high-growth markets.”

Outlook

  • Martin said guidance was unchanged: “performance in the first quarter was encouraging and in line with our expectations, resulting in no change in our guidance range and underlying assumptions with the small exception of a slightly lower share count resulting from our share repurchase activity.”

  • On the pace of improvement, Marr said, “We expect continued gradual improvement throughout 2026, albeit without a projected catalyst coming from the macro environment.”

  • In Q&A on guidance reiteration, Martin said, “nothing has really changed… nothing has happened in the last 60 days that has had an impact or a change on our overall view for the year.”

Financial results

  • Martin reported, “Same-store revenue growth was 0.6% over last year,” and added, “nice to see top line growth flip to positive for the first time since mid-2024.”

  • Margin performance reflected higher operating costs: “Revenue growth of 0.6% combined with 5.8% expense growth yielded negative 1.5% same-store NOI growth for the quarter.”

  • Martin said, “We reported FFO per share as adjusted of $0.63 for the quarter, which was at the high end of our guidance entering the quarter.”

  • On platform growth, Martin said, “we added 33 stores to the platform in the first quarter and ended the quarter with 854 third-party stores under management.”

Q&A

  • Samir Khanal, BofA Securities: Asked when higher advertising spend would translate to occupancy; Christopher Marr (CEO, President & Trustee) said marketing ROI has been “good,” and that “we're starting to see it fairly quickly in both rate and occupancy and would expect that trend to continue.”

  • Khanal, BofA Securities: Asked about New York demand and supply; Marr said New York benefits from “almost a complete absence of new supply in the outer boroughs,” while noting Manhattan has seen supply and “those stores… with… really small blocker-sized units are a little bit softer there.”

  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS: Asked about the “240% increase in net rentals”; Marr cited Q1 rentals down 1.8%, April rentals up about 1%, and said “Vacates were down 3.9% in the quarter.”

  • Ravi Vaidya, Mizuho: Asked about potential New York pricing restrictions; Marr said, “We have not engaged directly in those conversations,” and emphasized focus on “providing a good value.”

  • Nicholas Joseph, Citigroup: Asked about leverage for buybacks; Martin said repurchases have been funded with “roughly $100 million in free cash flow” and that it’s “not super appealing for us to just lever up the balance sheet,” pointing instead to potential asset contributions to co-ownership vehicles.

  • Viktor Fediv, Scotiabank: Asked about refinancing the September 2026 maturity; Martin said CubeSmart is evaluating “likely either a 7- or a 10-year bond,” with indicative pricing “right around 5” for 7-years and “low to pushing mid-5” for 10-years.

  • Brendan Lynch, Barclays: Asked how large language models (LLMs) affect customer acquisition; Marr said LLM-driven conversions are “only about 1% or 2%” so far and called monetization “not quite there yet either.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, concentrating on marketing ROI, guidance conservatism, refinancing costs, regulation risk in New York, and the durability of Sunbelt improvements.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and measured in Q&A, with Marr saying, “We are confident,” while repeatedly characterizing improvements as “gradual” and emphasizing ongoing weekly trade-offs between rate and occupancy.

  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s message progressed from “fundamentals have stabilized” (Q4) to seeing “the inflection in same-store revenue growth” (Q1), while analysts shifted from supply/guidance framing toward more tactical questions on marketing efficiency, AI channels, and balance-sheet options.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 reflected a shift to positive same-store revenue growth (0.6%) from Q4’s “negative 0.1%,” while same-store expense growth accelerated (5.8%) from Q4’s “2.9%.”

  • FFO per share as adjusted moved to $0.63 in Q1 from $0.64 in Q4, with Q1 described by Martin as “at the high end of our guidance entering the quarter.”

  • Capital allocation commentary remained consistent quarter-to-quarter around buybacks and the public/private valuation gap, with Q1 adding that the company “closed on the first store” in the CBRE IM JV.

Risks and concerns

  • Marr cited the absence of a macro tailwind: “without a projected catalyst coming from the macro environment,” and noted continued uneven market performance tied to supply.

  • Cost pressure remained a focus, with Martin pointing to elevated snow removal, front-loaded marketing, and personnel expenses that he expects to “grow at current levels throughout the first half of the year.”

  • Regulation and policy uncertainty surfaced in Q&A around potential New York pricing restrictions; Marr said CubeSmart has not engaged directly and will “continue to be involved as appropriate.”

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as confirming late-2025 stabilization and delivering an operational turn toward positive same-store revenue growth, while expense inflation—particularly weather, marketing timing, and staffing—kept same-store NOI negative. Guidance was reaffirmed with only a share-count adjustment from repurchases, and management emphasized gradual improvement through 2026 driven by narrowing occupancy gaps, firmer move-in pricing, and easing supply headwinds, alongside continued capital allocation toward buybacks and the $250 million CBRE IM joint venture as an alternative path for portfolio growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wabash projects Q2 revenue of $380M-$400M as it targets over 25% dry van share
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Wabash National Corporation (WNC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "As we entered the first quarter, we did so with a clear-eyed view of the environment in front of us," Brent Yeagy (President, CEO & Director) said, adding, "Freight markets were uncertain and customers continue to act cautiously."
  • Yeagy said visibility improved entering Q2 and framed 2027 as a key inflection: "Now as we move into the second quarter of 2026, both our customers and our visibility continues to improve... [setting] up for a constructive 2027 as spot rates, contract rates, capacity and demand, all are coming together."
  • Yeagy highlighted demand signals and backlog: "As evidenced by the 19% increase in backlog versus prior quarter to $837 million," while also noting that "geopolitical uncertainty continues to influence customer behavior."
  • Yeagy reiterated cost actions and portfolio review: "The plant idling actions announced in our January 2026 goal are progressing as planned, with $3 million of the costs referenced in our prior call recognized in Q1 2026," and said the company continues to evaluate "opportunities to rationalize our portfolio and rightsize fixed costs."
  • Patrick Keslin (Senior VP & CFO) said results were pressured by volume and inefficiency: "For the first quarter of 2026, consolidated revenue was $303 million, coming in slightly below the low end of our prior guidance range," and "Lower production volumes continue to pressure operating efficiency."
  • Keslin quantified profitability and cash flow pressure: "Adjusted non-GAAP gross margin was negative 2.6% of sales and adjusted non-GAAP operating margin was negative 18.3%," while "Operating cash flow for the quarter was negative $33.7 million resulting in negative free cash flow of negative $37.3 million."

Outlook

  • Yeagy said Wabash will stay on quarterly guidance cadence: "Until then, we will continue to provide quarterly guidance only as we navigate this transitionary period."
  • Yeagy provided Q2 guidance and reiterated a sequential recovery setup: "For the second quarter, we expect revenue in the range of $380 million to $400 million and adjusted EPS in the range of negative $0.40 per share and negative $0.60 per share," and said, "This outlook is consistent with our expectation that Q1 2026 represented the low point for the year."
  • Keslin added an operating profitability marker and second-half expectation: "Looking ahead to the second quarter, we expect... an operating margin of approximately negative 5%," and "positive adjusted EBITDA expected in the second half of 2026."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s guide-only posture, management again emphasized limited visibility, with Yeagy noting in Q1: "Our near-term demand outlook remains balanced," while pointing to a "constructive 2027."

Financial Results

  • Keslin said Q1 volume and earnings were below internal expectations: "Adjusted non-GAAP net income attributable to common shareholders was negative $47.5 million or negative $1.17 per diluted share. These results were below expectations, driven primarily by lower than planned volumes."
  • Keslin described segment performance and a near-term drag from new upfit site startup activity: "Transportation Solutions generated $250 million in revenue and reported an operating loss of $34.5 million," and "Parts and Services delivered $54 million in revenue and negative $2 million of operating income... as we incurred stock costs for newly established upfit sites that have not yet begun generating revenue."
  • On liquidity and capital returns, Keslin said, "As of March 31, total liquidity, including cash and available borrowings was $165 million," and "returned $3.5 million to shareholders through our quarterly dividend."

Q&A

  • Michael Shlisky, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division: asked whether Q2 guidance assumed backlog was fully booked; Brent Yeagy (President, CEO & Director) responded, "We have complete visibility into the backlogs that went into our guidance."
  • Shlisky, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division: asked what needs to improve for truck bodies; Yeagy said truck bodies are impacted "Class 3 all the way up to predominantly Class 6" and pointed to macro triggers including that "Housing is a substantial part of the equation."
  • Shlisky, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division: asked about reefer plans and ramp readiness; Yeagy said on dry vans, "the relative hiring needs that we'll have on the early stages of the ramp are somewhat muted," and on refrigerated, "we are still going down the process of development of a repositioned refrigerated van product."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were focused on near-term demand certainty and lagging end-markets, pressing on backlog coverage, truck body macro drivers, and readiness to ramp, including Shlisky’s question on whether management was "still kind of waiting on a few orders."
  • Management tone was slightly positive on cycle progression but cautious on timing, with Yeagy emphasizing improving visibility ("customers are increasingly engaging") while maintaining guarded framing on uncertainty ("Freight markets were uncertain").
  • Versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on limited visibility and weak near-term demand, current-quarter tone leaned more constructive on 2027 setup, with Yeagy shifting from "early incremental signs of stabilization" (Q4) to "visibility continues to improve" (Q1).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management changes were explicit: Yeagy opened by recognizing, "Mike Pettit, who as of April 8, is transitioning out of Wabash," following Q4 participation where Pettit spoke as Chief Growth Officer.
  • Guidance posture stayed quarterly-only, but the guided period rolled forward from Q1 in the prior call to Q2 in the current call; Q1 results were described by Keslin as "below expectations," while management maintained the same directional framing that the first quarter marked the year’s low point.
  • Strategy messaging evolved from downturn cost alignment (Q4 idling actions and charges) to a mix of cost discipline plus market-share ambitions, with Yeagy stating in Q1, "The synergies from these initiatives lead us to target dry van share of more than 25% in the first half of the cycle."

Risks and Concerns

  • Yeagy said customer behavior remains constrained by uncertainty: "Fleets remaining conservative, extending asset wise and prioritizing flexibility over expansion," and added that "geopolitical uncertainty continues to influence customer behavior."
  • Keslin flagged a delayed recovery in truck bodies versus trailers: "we now expect this segment to remain soft through the first half of 2026, with a recovery profile that trails dry vans by approximately 6 to 9 months."
  • Liquidity and cash burn remained a focus, with Keslin citing negative operating cash flow and saying the company is "engaged in discussions with our banking partners" to address the ABL facility ahead of September 2026.

Final Takeaway

Management positioned Q1 as the low point of 2026 and guided to sequential improvement in Q2, while stressing continued uncertainty in customer capital spending. Leadership emphasized cost alignment and liquidity protection alongside a more constructive 2027 narrative, pointing to rising backlog, improving freight indicators, and policy-related tailwinds (Section 232 and pending trade actions) as supportive of future demand and pricing stability, while acknowledging that truck bodies may recover later than dry vans and that near-term profitability remains pressured by low volumes and startup costs at new upfit sites.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Stocks rise to fresh record highs as May trading starts with oil falling
data.description:
The stock market advanced to a new intraday record high Friday as Wall Street looked to kick off the month of May on an optimistic note, with oil prices retreating and corporate earnings going strong. 

The S&P 500 (SP500) rose 0.5% after closing above the 7.2K level for the first time ever in the prior session, and the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) -- crossing the 25K mark for the first time—gained 1.1%. Meanwhile, the Dow (DJI) ticked down 0.1%.

U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures (CL1:COM) gapped down 3.5% to $101.66 a barrel after Iran reportedly submitted its response via Pakistani mediators to the latest U.S. amendments to a draft deal to end the Middle East war. International benchmark Brent crude futures (CO1:COM) also dipped 1.7% to $108.53 a barrel, as the news revived hopes for an agreement to end the conflict. Still, while U.S. President Donald Trump said later Friday he is “not satisfied” with the new offer.

Meanwhile, Apple (AAPL) was in the spotlight, with its shares advancing 4.5% after the technology giant turned in better-than-expected results for fiscal Q2, as the iPhone 17 line continued to impress consumers.

Investors were also assessing fresh reads on U.S. manufacturing, with April S&P U.S. Manufacturing PMI rising slightly more than expected from a month earlier. The separate U.S. ISM Manufacturing PMI was unchanged in April and fell short of the consensus, with several components of the measure pointing to a stagflationary environment.

U.S. Treasury yields were mixed across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was roughly flat at 4.38%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.89%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) was little changed at 4.97%.
